description: Zahlen und Argumente rund um die Kernenergie aus dem Republik-Artikel "Atomkraft – eine alte neue Hoffnung" (Arian Bastani, 12.11.2019, Serie Energie der Zukunft, Teil 4). Die im Text genannten und verlinkten Werte sind als Werte mit Quellen modelliert; zwei einfache Rechnungen (CO2-Intensitätsverhältnis Deutschland/Grossbritannien und das hohe Photovoltaik-Abfall-Szenario) sind als calc-validation hinterlegt. Quelle des Artikels: https://www.republik.ch/2019/11/12/atomkraft-eine-alte-neue-hoffnung

words.name: nuclear
words.description: relating to the nucleus of an atom and the energy released in nuclear reactions.

words.name: power
words.description: the rate, per unit time, at which electrical energy is transferred.

words.name: plant
words.description: an industrial facility for the generation of electric power.

words.name: electricity
words.description: the set of physical phenomena associated with the presence and motion of electric charge.

words.name: production
words.description: the action of making or generating something.

words.name: reactor
words.description: a device in which a nuclear chain reaction is sustained to produce energy.

words.name: fast
words.description: of a nuclear reactor, sustaining the chain reaction with fast (unmoderated) neutrons.

words.name: fission
words.description: the splitting of a heavy atomic nucleus, releasing energy, used in current reactors.

words.name: fusion
words.description: the merging of light atomic nuclei, releasing energy, the process powering the sun.

words.name: intensity
words.description: the amount of a quantity per unit of another, here emissions per unit of energy.

words.name: emission
words.description: the production and discharge of something, especially a gas.

words.name: lifecycle
words.description: the whole series of stages over the life of a product or facility.

words.name: share
words.description: a part or portion of a larger amount.

words.name: energy
words.description: the capacity to do work, here primary or electrical energy.

words.name: primary
words.description: of energy, in the raw form before conversion (e.g. fuel rather than electricity).

words.name: wind
words.description: the use of air flow to generate electricity.

words.name: solar
words.description: relating to energy from the sun.

words.name: photovoltaic
words.description: converting light directly into electricity.

words.name: coal
words.description: a combustible black sedimentary rock burned to generate electricity.

words.name: turbine
words.description: a rotary machine that converts a flow into work, here a wind turbine.

words.name: capacity
words.description: the maximum output an installation can produce.

words.name: installed
words.description: built and connected, ready to produce.

words.name: cost
words.description: the amount paid or required for something.

words.name: construction
words.description: the building of an installation.

words.name: time
words.description: the measured duration of an event.

words.name: delay
words.description: a period by which something is late or postponed.

words.name: rate
words.description: a quantity measured against another, here change per unit time.

words.name: ramp
words.description: the adjustment of a power plant's output to follow demand.

words.name: death
words.description: the end of life, here counted as fatalities of an energy source.

words.name: toll
words.description: the total number of deaths caused by an event or technology.

words.name: waste
words.description: unwanted or unusable material; here spent nuclear fuel or end-of-life panels.

words.name: radioactive
words.description: emitting ionising radiation through nuclear decay.

words.name: decommissioning
words.description: the process of shutting down and dismantling a facility.

words.name: multiplier
words.description: a factor by which a quantity is multiplied.

words.name: uranium
words.description: a chemical element used as nuclear fuel.

words.name: uranium 235
words.description: the fissile isotope of uranium used in current reactors.

words.name: natural
words.description: occurring in nature, here unenriched uranium.

words.name: fuel
words.description: material consumed to produce energy.

words.name: enriched
words.description: of uranium, processed to raise the share of the fissile isotope.

words.name: weapon
words.description: relating to a nuclear weapon, requiring highly enriched uranium.

words.name: reserve
words.description: the known stock of a resource available for use.

words.name: duration
words.description: the length of time something continues or lasts.

words.name: operation
words.description: the running of a plant, here measured in reactor-years.

words.name: cumulative
words.description: accumulated over all units and years.

words.name: salt
words.description: an ionic compound; here the coolant of a molten salt reactor.

words.name: molten
words.description: liquefied by heat.

words.name: temperature
words.description: the degree of heat of an operating reactor.

words.name: start
words.description: the beginning of an activity, here an experiment.

words.name: experiment
words.description: a scientific trial, here of nuclear fusion.

words.name: exposure
words.description: the amount of ionising radiation received.

words.name: radiation
words.description: energy emitted as particles or waves, here ionising radiation.

words.name: warming
words.description: the rise of global average temperature.

words.name: global
words.description: worldwide in scope.

words.name: growth
words.description: an increase in amount or size.

words.name: scenario
words.description: a modelled possible future, here an IPCC climate scenario.

words.name: factor
words.description: a ratio; here the multiple by which a quantity changes.

words.name: ratio
words.description: the quotient of two quantities.

words.name: price
words.description: the amount of money expected or given for something.

words.name: carbon
words.description: shorthand for carbon dioxide in the context of a carbon price.

words.name: lower bound estimate
words.description: the low end of an estimated range.

words.name: upper bound estimate
words.description: the high end of an estimated range.

words.name: Germany
words.description: a country in central Europe.

words.name: United Kingdom
words.description: a country in north-western Europe.

words.name: France
words.description: a country in western Europe with a large nuclear fleet.

words.name: Finland
words.description: a country in northern Europe building the Olkiluoto III reactor.

words.name: Denmark
words.description: a country in northern Europe with a large wind fleet.

words.name: Chooz B2
words.description: a French nuclear reactor.

words.name: Hinkley Point
words.description: a British nuclear power station under construction.

words.name: Olkiluoto III
words.description: a long-delayed Finnish EPR reactor.

words.name: Gösgen
words.description: a Swiss nuclear power station in the canton of Solothurn.

words.name: EBR-II
words.description: the US Experimental Breeder Reactor II, a fast reactor.

words.name: Chernobyl
words.description: the site of the 1986 nuclear accident in Ukraine.

words.name: Banqiao dam
words.description: a Chinese dam whose 1975 failure caused a major death toll.

words.name: gram
words.description: a metric unit of mass.

words.name: kWh
words.description: kilowatt-hour, a unit of energy.

words.name: TWh
words.description: terawatt-hour, a unit of energy equal to one billion kilowatt-hours.

words.name: GW
words.description: gigawatt, a unit of power equal to one billion watts.

words.name: percent
words.description: a proportion expressed as a fraction of 100.

words.name: year
words.description: a unit of duration equal to the orbital period of the Earth.

words.name: tonne
words.description: a metric unit of mass equal to 1000 kilograms.

words.name: EUR
words.description: the euro.

words.name: GBP
words.description: the pound sterling.

words.name: CO2
words.description: carbon dioxide, the principal anthropogenic greenhouse gas.

words.name: minute
words.description: a unit of time equal to sixty seconds.

words.name: person
words.description: a single human being, the unit of a death toll.

words.name: times
words.description: a dimensionless multiplier or factor.

words.name: count
words.description: a dimensionless number of countable items.

words.name: Celsius
words.description: degree Celsius, a unit of temperature.

words.name: million
words.description: a scaling word for 1e6.

words.name: bn
words.description: billion, a scaling word for 1e9.
the calendar year 2000.
the calendar year 2017.
the calendar year 2018.
the calendar year 2019.
the calendar year 1975.
the calendar year 2050.
the calendar year 2100.
the calendar year 2025.

triples.name: nuclear power plant
triples.description: a thermal power station in which the heat source is a nuclear reactor.

triples.name: electricity CO2 intensity
triples.description: the carbon-dioxide emitted per unit of electricity generated.

triples.name: Germany to UK CO2 intensity ratio
triples.description: how many times more carbon-intensive German electricity is than British electricity; the result of the Germany to UK CO2 intensity ratio formula.

triples.name: nuclear electricity production share
triples.description: the share of total electricity production supplied by nuclear power.

triples.name: global primary energy share
triples.description: the share of worldwide primary energy supplied by a source.

triples.name: photovoltaic waste high scenario
triples.description: the projected photovoltaic waste if two thirds of global energy came from solar panels; the result of the photovoltaic waste high scenario formula.

triples.name: natural uranium share
triples.description: the share of the fissile isotope uranium 235 in natural uranium.

triples.name: enriched fuel share
triples.description: the share of the fissile isotope uranium 235 in enriched reactor fuel.

triples.name: weapon uranium share
triples.description: the share of the fissile isotope uranium 235 in weapons-grade uranium.

triples.name: molten salt reactor
triples.description: a reactor cooled by liquid salt at high temperature and near atmospheric pressure.

triples.name: scenario growth factor
triples.description: the multiple by which a source grows in a climate scenario.

triples.name: lifecycle CO2 emission
triples.description: carbon-dioxide emissions over the full lifecycle of a generation technology, per unit of energy.

triples.name: lifecycle CO2 emission of nuclear power

triples.description: grams of CO2-equivalent emitted per kilowatt-hour generated.
percent of nominal power per minute, a power-plant ramp rate.

triples.name: EUR per tonne CO2
triples.description: euros per tonne of CO2, a carbon price.

sources.name: Republik Atomkraft 2019
sources.description: Arian Bastani, "Atomkraft – eine alte neue Hoffnung", Republik, 12.11.2019 (Serie Energie der Zukunft, Teil 4).

sources.name: Electric Insights UK 2018
sources.description: The state of Britain's power system in 2018: average grid CO2 intensity around 217 g/kWh.

sources.name: Umweltbundesamt CO2 Strom
sources.description: German federal environment agency: CO2 emissions per kilowatt-hour of electricity, about 474 g/kWh.

sources.name: Statista Atomstrom DE
sources.description: Share of nuclear energy in German electricity generation (about one eighth).

sources.name: World Nuclear Chooz B-2
sources.description: Reactor data for the French Chooz B2 unit, about 12 TWh produced in 2018.

sources.name: State of Green Denmark 2018
sources.description: 2018 was a great year for Danish wind energy: over 6000 turbines, about 14 TWh.

sources.name: BP Statistical Review 2019
sources.description: Global energy mix; nuclear share of primary energy peaked near 6% around 2000 and fell below 5%.

sources.name: IAEA PRIS reactor age
sources.description: IAEA Power Reactor Information System: about 449 operational reactors, most 30-40 years old.

sources.name: Guardian Hinkley Point cost
sources.description: Hinkley Point C expected to cost over 20 billion pounds.

sources.name: Reuters Olkiluoto
sources.description: Areva's Finnish Olkiluoto III reactor, about 10 billion euro and a 15-year build with repeated delays.

sources.name: Guardian Hinkley delay
sources.description: Hinkley Point C rising costs and long delays, at least seven years late.

sources.name: OECD NEA carbon pricing 2011
sources.description: OECD Nuclear Energy Agency: a carbon price of 30 to 50 euro per tonne would make nuclear competitive.

sources.name: Framatome Gösgen flexibility
sources.description: A 2017 system at the Gösgen plant adjusts output to demand, ramping up to 10% of nominal power per minute.

sources.name: IEA WEO 2018 electricity
sources.description: IEA World Energy Outlook 2018: photovoltaic waste estimated at almost 80 million tonnes by 2050.

sources.name: IPCC SR15 Chapter 2
sources.description: IPCC Special Report on 1.5C, Chapter 2: pathways and the current-policy path towards about 3C warming.

sources.name: World Nuclear IPCC 1.5C
sources.description: World Nuclear Association on the IPCC 1.5C report: nuclear about two and a half times today in the scenario.

sources.name: IPCC AR5 Annex III
sources.description: IPCC AR5 WG3 Annex III lifecycle GHG emission medians (g CO2eq/kWh): nuclear 12, solar PV utility 48.

values.note: German electricity is well over double as carbon-intensive as British electricity.
about one eighth of German electricity comes from nuclear power.

words: lifecycle CO2 emission of nuclear power

values.note: over 6000 turbines; the French Chooz B2 generated over 5000 times more than one Danish turbine.
12 TWh / (14 TWh / 6000 turbines) = 5143; the article rounds this to over 5000 times.
the peak share of nuclear power in the global primary energy mix, around the turn of the millennium.
two years before the 2019 article the share had fallen below 5 percent.
about 449 operational reactors worldwide, half of them in the US and Europe, most 30-40 years old.
over 20 billion pounds, more than double the 2012 London Olympics.
projects in Finland and France each cost around 10 billion euro.
build times are mostly under 10 years, but the trend is rising.
Hinkley Point will feed in power at least seven years late.
during the 15-year Olkiluoto III build, nearly 2 GW of wind was installed in Finland, more than the reactor will deliver.
a CO2 price of 30 to 50 euro per tonne would let nuclear compete on the market.
at high load the output can be changed by up to 10 percent of nominal power per minute.
Chernobyl claimed about 4000 deaths according to the World Health Organization.
the 1975 Banqiao dam failures in China killed about 230,000 people.
radiation exposure from coal power is about 100 times greater than from nuclear power.
highly radioactive waste accumulated worldwide over 70 years, about 250,000 tonnes.
of the decommissioning material, 90 percent is only weakly radioactive.
the IEA estimates photovoltaic waste at almost 80 million tonnes by 2050.
if two thirds of global energy came from solar panels the waste could be fifteen times higher.
current reactors fission only about 4 percent of the uranium in the fuel rods.
uranium 235 makes up less than 1 percent of natural uranium.
enriched reactor fuel is about 5 percent uranium 235.
weapons need over 90 percent uranium 235.
known uranium reserves last over 130 years at a constant consumption rate (OECD).
over 400 reactor-years of fast-reactor operation have been collected in 20 reactors.
30 of those years were at the US EBR-II reactor.
no extra pressure is needed to keep the salt liquid at over 300 degrees Celsius.
fusion requires temperatures around 150 million degrees Celsius.
fusion experiments are to begin in France in 2025, with an uncertain outcome.
the IPCC 1.5C scenario sketches two and a half times as much nuclear energy as today.
the current path leads to a roughly 3 degrees Celsius hotter world by the end of the 21st century.

formulas.name: Germany to UK CO2 intensity ratio formula
formulas.expression: "Germany to UK CO2 intensity ratio" = "Germany electricity CO2 intensity" / "United Kingdom electricity CO2 intensity"

formulas.name: photovoltaic waste high scenario formula
formulas.expression: "photovoltaic waste high scenario" = "photovoltaic waste" * "solar dominance multiplier"

calc-validation.formula: Germany to UK CO2 intensity ratio formula
words: Germany to UK CO2 intensity ratio
calc-validation.note: German electricity (474 g/kWh) divided by British electricity (217 g/kWh) = 2.18, matching the article's well over double.
80 million tonnes of projected photovoltaic waste times the fifteen-fold solar-dominance factor = 1200 million tonnes.
